body.label: Definition 1
body.definition: Let G be a group with identity element e. A function l : G -> R is a pseudo-length function on G if the following four conditions hold: nonnegativity, identity, symmetry, and triangle inequality.
body.name: PseudoLength

source.text: Let G be a group with identity element e. A function l : G -> R is a pseudo-length function on G if the following four conditions hold: nonnegativity, identity, symmetry, and triangle inequality.
source.label: Definition 1

body.text: The nonnegativity condition is included explicitly so that the codomain can be the usual real numbers in a formalization. Equivalently, one may take the codomain to be the set of nonnegative real numbers and omit the separate nonnegativity axiom.

body.label: Definition 2
body.definition: Let G be a group with identity element e. A pseudo-length function l : G -> R is a length function if, for every g in G, g != e implies 0 < l(g).
body.name: IsLength

source.text: Let G be a group with identity element e. A pseudo-length function l : G -> R is a length function if, for every g in G, g != e implies 0 < l(g).
source.label: Definition 2

body.label: Definition 3
body.definition: Let G be a group. A pseudo-length function l : G -> R is homogeneous if, for every g in G and every integer n, l(g^n) = |n| l(g).
body.name: IsHomogeneousPseudoLength

source.text: Let G be a group. A pseudo-length function l : G -> R is homogeneous if, for every g in G and every integer n, l(g^n) = |n| l(g).
source.label: Definition 3

body.text: In particular, for every g in G, homogeneity gives l(g^2)=2l(g).

proof.text: Fix x,y in G. We first prove l(yxy^{-1}) <= l(x). Let n be a positive integer. By homogeneity, n l(yxy^{-1}) = l((yxy^{-1})^n). The group identity (yxy^{-1})^n = yx^n y^{-1} gives n l(yxy^{-1}) = l(yx^n y^{-1}). By the triangle inequality, l(yx^n y^{-1}) <= l(y) + l(x^n) + l(y^{-1}). By symmetry, l(y^{-1})=l(y). By homogeneity, l(x^n)=n l(x). Hence n l(yxy^{-1}) <= 2l(y)+n l(x). Dividing by n gives l(yxy^{-1}) <= l(x)+2l(y)/n. Since this holds for every positive integer n and 2l(y)/n tends to 0, l(yxy^{-1}) <= l(x). For the reverse inequality, define z := yxy^{-1}. Then x=y^{-1}zy. Applying the inequality already proved with z in place of x and y^{-1} in place of y gives l(x)=l(y^{-1}zy) <= l(z)=l(yxy^{-1}). The two inequalities imply equality.

proof.text: Let n be a positive integer. From the hypotheses and the identity for powers of conjugates, a^n=s(wy)^n s^{-1} and a^n=t(zw^{-1})^n t^{-1}. Hence a^{2n}=s(wy)^n s^{-1}t(zw^{-1})^n t^{-1}. By homogeneity, 2n l(a)=l(a^{2n}). The triangle inequality and Lemma 2 give l(a^{2n}) <= l(s)+l((wy)^n s^{-1}t(zw^{-1})^n)+l(t^{-1}) <= l(s)+l(s^{-1})+l(t)+n(l(y)+l(z))+l(t^{-1}). By symmetry this is 2l(s)+2l(t)+n(l(y)+l(z)). Dividing by 2n gives l(a) <= (l(y)+l(z))/2 + (l(s)+l(t))/n. Letting n tend to infinity gives l(a) <= (l(y)+l(z))/2.

proof_steps.proof_method: Simplify KtwoN = n - sum_{i=1}^{2n} (1 + epsilon_i)/2 = n - (2n)/2 - (epsilon_1 + ... + epsilon_{2n})/2, then use the definition of S_{2n}.

proof.text: Lemma 4 gives f(m,k) <= 1/2 f(m-1,k)+1/2 f(m+1,k-1). Equivalently, for a Rademacher variable epsilon, f(m,k) <= E[f(m+epsilon,k-(1+epsilon)/2)]. Starting at (0,n) and applying this successively with epsilon_1,...,epsilon_{2n} gives f(0,n) <= E[f(M_{2n},K_{2n})], where M_{2n}=epsilon_1+...+epsilon_{2n} and K_{2n}=n-sum_{i=1}^{2n}(1+epsilon_i)/2. Then M_{2n}=S_{2n} and K_{2n}=-S_{2n}/2, so the result follows.

proof.text: Fix x,y in G and define c:=[x,y]. Let n be a positive integer. By homogeneity, n l(c)=l(c^n)=f(0,n), where f(m,k):=l(x^m c^k). By Lemma 5, f(0,n)<=E[f(S_{2n},-S_{2n}/2)]. For every even integer r, f(r,-r/2)=l(x^r c^{-r/2}). By the triangle inequality, l(x^r c^{-r/2})<=l(x^r)+l(c^{-r/2}). By homogeneity these are |r|l(x) and |r|l(c)/2. Thus E[f(S_{2n},-S_{2n}/2)] <= E(|S_{2n}|)(l(x)+l(c)/2). Lemma 6 gives E(|S_{2n}|)<=sqrt(2n). Hence n l(c)<=sqrt(2n)(l(x)+l(c)/2). Dividing by n and letting n tend to infinity gives l(c)<=0. Nonnegativity gives 0<=l(c), so l(c)=0. Since x and y were arbitrary, the result follows.

proof_steps.claim: lengthFromNorm is a homogeneous length function on G.
proof_steps.proof_method: These verified properties are exactly the axioms of a homogeneous length function.

proof_steps.text: Injectivity and the norm give positivity away from 0; the norm axioms give l(0)=0, l(-g)=l(g), l(g+h)<=l(g)+l(h), and l(ng)=|n|l(g). Thus l is a homogeneous length function on G.
